Text Stroke (Halo)¶

Text stroke draws an outline around text. It is useful when labels are placed on top of colorful, textured, or busy plot areas and the label color alone does not give enough contrast.

Use stroke to set the outline width and stroke_color to set the halo color.

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd

from lets_plot import *
from lets_plot.bistro.corr import corr_plot

LetsPlot.setup_html()
In [2]:
mpg = (
    pd.read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JetBrains/lets-plot-docs/master/data/mpg.csv')
    .drop(columns=['Unnamed: 0'])
)

mpg_num = mpg.select_dtypes(include=np.number)
mpg_num.head(3)
Out[2]:
displ year cyl cty hwy
0 1.8 1999 4 18 29
1 1.8 1999 4 21 29
2 2.0 2008 4 20 31

Correlation Plot Labels¶

A dark stroke keeps white labels readable over tiles with different fill colors.

In [3]:
gggrid([
    corr_plot(mpg_num)
    .tiles()
    .labels(color='white')
    .build(),

    corr_plot(mpg_num)
    .tiles()
    .labels(color='white', stroke=1.6, stroke_color='gray40')
    .build()
])
Out[3]:

Compare Text Labels With and Without Stroke¶

The same scatter plot can be reused to compare plain text labels with labels that have a white halo. The stroke helps preserve the label color while separating the text from nearby points.

In [4]:
sample = (
    mpg.sort_values('hwy', ascending=False)
    .groupby('manufacturer', as_index=False)
    .head(1)
)

base_plot = (
    ggplot(mpg, aes('displ', 'hwy'))
    + geom_point(aes(color='class'), size=4, alpha=.75)
    + theme_minimal()
    + ggsize(520, 360)
)

label_options = dict(
    data=sample,
    color='gray10',
    size=7,
    check_overlap=True,
)

no_stroke = (
    base_plot
    + geom_text(aes(label='manufacturer'), **label_options)
    + ggtitle('Labels without stroke')
)

with_stroke = (
    base_plot
    + geom_text(aes(label='manufacturer'), stroke=1.5, stroke_color='white', **label_options)
    + ggtitle('Labels with white halo')
)

gggrid([no_stroke, with_stroke], ncol=1)
Out[4]:

Repelled Labels on a Map¶

For geographic labels, a light halo separates place names from map outlines and neighboring markers while keeping the map styling simple.

In [5]:
base_url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JetBrains/lets-plot-kotlin/master/docs/examples/shp/'
world_gdf = gpd.read_file(base_url + 'naturalearth_lowres/naturalearth_lowres.shp')
cities_gdf = gpd.read_file(base_url + 'naturalearth_cities/naturalearth_cities.shp')

(
    ggplot()
    + geom_map(map=world_gdf, fill='light_green', alpha=.3)
    + geom_point(map=cities_gdf, color='dark_slate_gray', size=3)
    + geom_text_repel(
        aes(label='name'),
        data=cities_gdf,
        color='dark_slate_gray',
        seed=42,
        max_iter=200,
        stroke=2.0,
        stroke_color='white',
    )
    + coord_map(xlim=[-10.5, 44.0], ylim=[37.0, 60.5])
    + theme(axis='blank', panel_grid='blank')
    + ggsize(800, 600)
)
Out[5]:
In [ ]:
